narrative_blocks.header: The quiet sister among soldiers and writers
narrative_blocks.body: Born between her decorated father's two wars, she lived more privately than her siblings and married into the Jackson family. She outlived all but the youngest of them.

narrative_blocks.header: The quiet sister among soldiers and writers
narrative_blocks.body: Born between her decorated father's two wars, she lived more privately than her siblings and married into the Jackson family. She outlived all but the youngest of them.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Nuremberg prosecutor
was her mother-in-law, who lived to ninety-five

narrative_blocks.header: He was his father's right hand at Nuremberg
narrative_blocks.body: Fresh from Harvard Law and the Navy, he served as personal assistant to his father, the chief American prosecutor of the Nazi leadership. That trial was the first case of his career.

narrative_blocks.header: A lifetime at one great firm
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his whole career at Milbank, Tweed, rising to chair the firm and head its litigation. His clients ranged from Nelson Rockefeller to Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis and the New York Stock Exchange.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued for a permanent war-crimes court
narrative_blocks.body: He edited the published record of the Nuremberg evidence and pressed in print for a standing international criminal court decades before one existed. He twice argued before the Supreme Court.

cross_connections.display_label: served the Rockefellers from Milbank Tweed too

narrative_blocks.header: Travel consultant and Roosevelt great-granddaughter
narrative_blocks.body: A graduate of Chapin and New York University, she worked as a travel consultant and was one of five Jackson daughters. She married Thomas White Hook in a ceremony followed by a reception at the River Club.

narrative_blocks.header: From the Peace Corps to Madison Avenue
narrative_blocks.body: He served two years as a Peace Corps volunteer in Liberia before joining a New York advertising agency. He married a great-granddaughter of Theodore Roosevelt.

notable.notable_blurb: Literary agent to Pynchon and DeLillo

narrative_blocks.header: She represents the author no one can find
narrative_blocks.body: Melanie Jackson founded the Melanie Jackson Agency, representing a distinguished roster of authors including Nobel laureate Wole Soyinka, Pulitzer winners Ron Chernow and Steve Coll, and novelist Percival Everett. Her most famous client is the reclusive Thomas Pynchon.

narrative_blocks.header: Her most elusive client became her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jackson began representing Thomas Pynchon in the 1980s, after the famously private novelist left his longtime agent. The two married in 1990 and have guarded their privacy ever since.

cross_connections.display_label: was her grandfather, the chief American prosecutor at Nuremberg

notable.notable_blurb: Novelist; Gravity’s Rainbow; National Book Award 1974

education.notes: Engineering physics, then English; attended Nabokov’s classes

narrative_blocks.header: Two years in the Navy, then V.
narrative_blocks.body: Out of Oyster Bay High School in 1953, Pynchon went to Cornell, served two years in the Navy from 1955, and published his first novel, V., in 1963 — the beginning of one of American fiction’s most private and most celebrated careers.

narrative_blocks.header: Gravity’s Rainbow, then the genius grant
narrative_blocks.body: Gravity’s Rainbow took the National Book Award for Fiction in 1974, and the MacArthur Fellowship followed in 1988. The encyclopedic novelist descends in a straight line from the Guilford Ruggles parsonage — and from the Springfield Pynchons Hawthorne fictionalized.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote manuals for nuclear missiles
narrative_blocks.body: From 1960 to 1962 Pynchon worked at Boeing as a technical writer on the Bomarc and Minuteman nuclear missile programs — then quit, finished V., and never held an ordinary job again. Gravity’s Rainbow’s rockets had a paper trail.

narrative_blocks.header: A paper bag for his son’s sake
narrative_blocks.body: Only about ten public images of Pynchon exist — “‘Recluse’ is a code word generated by journalists,” he said, “meaning ‘doesn’t like to talk to reporters.’” But when his son Jackson loved The Simpsons, Pynchon voiced his own cameo, animated with a paper bag over his head.

cross_connections.display_label: was his wife’s grandfather — Justice and Nuremberg prosecutor

narrative_blocks.header: He found his father’s archive a home
narrative_blocks.body: Jackson compiled and represented the Pynchon archive in its placement at The Huntington — drawn, he said, to its aerospace and mathematics holdings and “extraordinary map collection.” The famously private novelist’s papers now sit beside rocket history.

notable.notable_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

narrative_blocks.header: The only man twice ruined by one wound
narrative_blocks.body: A grenade in New Guinea in 1943 shattered the same knee German fire had wrecked in France in 1918, leaving him the only American ever rated totally disabled for one wound in two wars. He came home with four Silver Stars.

narrative_blocks.header: The old lion is dead
narrative_blocks.body: When Theodore Roosevelt died in January 1919, Archibald wired the news to his scattered brothers and sister in five blunt words. He was still recovering from his French wounds at the time.

narrative_blocks.header: From Teapot Dome to a bond house
narrative_blocks.body: He resigned from Sinclair Oil during the Teapot Dome inquiry and, though never implicated, gave testimony damaging to his former employer. After the second war he founded the municipal-bond firm Roosevelt and Cross, which still trades today.

narrative_blocks.header: A hard turn to the far right
narrative_blocks.body: In his later years he joined the John Birch Society and founded the Veritas Foundation to root out what he called socialist influence at Harvard. In 1954 he published a pamphlet attacking the award of a Theodore Roosevelt medal to the diplomat Ralph Bunche.

narrative_blocks.header: The last of Edith's sons
narrative_blocks.body: He outlived every brother, the only son of Theodore and Edith to come through both World Wars alive. His stone at Oyster Bay reads, the old fighting man home from the wars.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

military_service.notes: Severely wounded in France and discharged with full disability; two Silver Star citations and the Croix de Guerre.
Commanded in New Guinea; wounded at the ridge named in his honor.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in the car her husband was driving
narrative_blocks.body: In 1971 she died in an automobile crash near the couple's pre-Revolutionary house on Turkey Lane in Cold Spring Harbor, with Archibald at the wheel. They had married at Boston's Emmanuel Church in 1917.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Archibald Roosevelt, TR's son

notable.notable_blurb: 26th US President; Rough Rider; Nobel Peace laureate

cross_connections.display_label: his Harvard ornithology partner on an 1877 bird list
Trust-busted Morgan's Northern Securities Company in 1904
designed the townhouse his family used near the White House
drew up the guardianship papers for his children
the Columbia president and Nobel laureate he befriended

military_service.notes: Led the assault on the San Juan Heights, 1898; Medal of Honor awarded in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the childhood friend next door
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt married Edith Kermit Carow in London in 1886, his second wife and a granddaughter of the Union general Daniel Tyler. Through her he entered the Tyler family, with its Edwards and Hooker roots in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard ornithology friendship became his first publication
narrative_blocks.body: He met Henry Davis Minot, a Boston-based naturalist, as a Harvard freshman in 1877. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — his first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Trust-busted J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities Company
narrative_blocks.body: His administration brought the antitrust suit that dissolved Morgan's Northern Securities holding company in 1904, ending the merger of the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads. Morgan reportedly told him 'Send your man to my man and they can fix it up' — the President refused, and the Supreme Court sided with the government.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Columbia's president Nicholas Butler
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's circle included Nicholas Murray Butler, the long-serving Columbia president and 1931 Nobel peace laureate, whom he nicknamed Nicholas Miraculous. The two Republican allies stayed close from Roosevelt's rise through the White House years.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelts borrowed a famous architect's townhouse
narrative_blocks.body: When the White House was torn up for renovation in 1902, the family decamped to a townhouse belonging to the firm of Carrère and Hastings. Its partner Thomas Hastings would go on to design the New York Public Library and Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.
